data.title: A Look At Guoquan Food (Shanghai) (SEHK:2517) Valuation As It Expands Into Alcohol Beverage Partnerships
data.description:
Advertisement

Alcohol partnership with Songhe puts Guoquan Food’s beverage plans in focus

Guoquan Food (Shanghai) (SEHK:2517) has signed an alcoholic beverage procurement agreement with Songhe Group, tying drinks more tightly to its hot pot and barbecue offerings across more than 11,000 retail stores.

See our latest analysis for Guoquan Food (Shanghai).

The new alcohol deal lands after a strong 1 year total shareholder return of 119.77%, even though recent momentum has softened. The 7 day share price return is 8.29% and the 90 day share price return is 5.15% from HK$3.87.

If this shift into beverages has you thinking about where else growth stories might emerge, it could be worth checking out our screener of 103 top founder-led companies as a starting point for fresh ideas.

With Guoquan trading at an intrinsic value estimate about 53% below its current price and roughly 27% below analyst targets, the key question is whether this discount signals a buying opportunity or whether the market is already pricing in potential future growth.

Price to earnings of 27.4x: Is it justified?

On a P/E of 27.4x, Guoquan Food (Shanghai) is priced above the wider Asian consumer retailing industry average of 17.5x, even after the recent share price pull back to HK$3.87.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price with earnings per share, so a higher multiple usually reflects the market paying more today for each unit of profit. For a home meal products and retail business like Guoquan, that often ties back to how confident investors are about future earnings, store economics and the sustainability of margin trends.

Here, the picture is mixed. On one hand, earnings have grown by 50.5% over the past year and by 48% per year over the past 5 years, with net profit margins currently at 4.7% compared with 3.6% last year. Analysts also expect earnings to grow about 26.5% per year over the next 3 years, which is faster than the wider Hong Kong market, and revenue growth of 16.2% per year is forecast to outpace the local market rate of 8.5%. On the other hand, the current 27.4x P/E is higher than the company’s own estimated fair P/E of 25.8x, and return on equity is 11%, which is flagged as low compared with a 20% benchmark.

Versus peers, Guoquan looks expensive against the broader Asian consumer retailing industry multiple of 17.5x. However, it screens as better value when compared to its direct peer group average multiple of 37.9x. That gap between the current 27.4x and the fair P/E estimate of 25.8x is also a level the market could move towards if sentiment or growth expectations change, so the current premium is not extreme but it is there.

Approach 1: BlueScope Steel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back into today’s money to arrive at an estimated intrinsic value per share.

For BlueScope Steel, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on A$ free cash flows. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about A$458.9m. Analysts provide specific forecasts through 2030, with A$1,536.0m of free cash flow projected in that year, and Simply Wall St extrapolates further years beyond the analyst horizon. When you total and adjust these discounted cash flows, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of A$65.44 per share.
